Morning.
Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee.

for your presentation and questions, so the floor is in your hands.
Way to go.
Thank you. So, yeah, thank you for the opportunity to present.
I'm Rebecca Fisher. I'm from Horticulture New Zealand.
With me is Chung Wa Shen, and from our risk policy team,
and we're joined by Simon Cook, a kiwifruit grower, an avocado grower from the Bay
of Plenty, and also a registered chemical applicator.
Simon will provide practical examples from a grower's perspective.
We will briefly highlight two key points from our submission and then welcome
questions, so we'll definitely stick to time.
As you all know, HortNZ represents over 4,300 fruit and vegetable
growers who contribute over 7.5 billion to our economy.
The sector is enabling to double farm gate value by
2035, but this depends on growers having access to
efficacious crop protection products.
HortNZ supports the government's objectives of improving growers'
access to crop protection products, and as such, we do support the
direction of reform.
Our recommendations are intended to help ensure the bill delivers the
outcomes it was designed to achieve, while maintaining robust
environmental practices
and protections.
So I'd like to focus on three areas today.
The first, recognizing products approved by trusted
regulators overseas, as the previous speaker sort of covered.
New Zealand is a country of 5 million people.
We cannot realistically replicate the scientific capability of much
larger countries, nor can we expect companies to undertake bespoke,
extensive regulatory processes for such a small market as ours.
Recognizing products approved by trusted overseas regulators is not
simply an efficiency measure, it is actually a practical necessity.
If a crop protection product is registered in a trusted country, the data
provided to the overseas regulator should satisfy
requirements for it to be registered in New Zealand without reanalyzing
the overseas data. This would eliminate duplication, as
was said on the screen there, of what our trusted countries have already
done. Any additional assessment to manage New Zealand risks should
be limited to genuine, specific New Zealand issues.
Global harmonization of crop protection products should be the default approach by
ACVM and EPA, as is done with human medicines, where Medsafe
leverages international assessments.
And as such, in our submission, we recommend establishing a standalone
international pathway for this that is mandatory with limited
exclusions.
So our second point,
application of the precautionary approach.
HortNZ supports the precautionary approach and strong environmental
protections. Our concern is not with protection itself,
but with ensuring it is applied proportionately and consistently.
Scientific uncertainty is inherent in all risk assessments, and
regulatory decisions are rarely made with complete certainty.
But it is important to distinguish between hazard and actual practical risk, and
between theoretical exposure pathways and likely exposure under
real-world conditions. It is also important to consider benefits
when assessing risks. We are not seeking lower standards, rather
greater clarity and consistency around how precaution is applied.
We recommend a policy framework is created that outlines the best course of action
if there is uncertainty, and sets out how precaution is applied in
practice, including to give effect to the full purposes of the
act, specifically economic, social, and cultural well-being.
Currently, EPA implements a very strict precautionary approach, which
influences decision-making processes, and we are asking for an approach that is
proportionate to risk.
A third and final point we'd like to emphasize is that HortNZ does not
support introducing a levy. Throughout our submission, we identify
opportunities to improve efficiency using global data,
reduced duplication of assessments and reassessments.
This all means better use of regulatory resources.
Utilizing the assessment done by overseas regulators will reduce
duplication and significantly reduce the workload and resource required.
This opportunity should be implemented first.
A
new levy should be a measure of last resort rather than the starting point,
after all avenues for efficiencies have been explored.
A full list of our recommended amendments are in our submission, and overall, we do
not see these as radical changes. Rather, they are practical changes intended
to help ensure New Zealand growers have timely access to the same modern tools
available as their international competitors, while maintaining robust
science-based environmental protections.
We'd like to encourage the committee to use this opportunity to ensure the bill
delivers meaningful benefits to growers to enable double farm gate
value, for our vision for our sector to be realized.
Thank you.
Thanks.

Thank you, Chair, and members. My name's Jodi Brunning.
I'm a member of the Physicians and Scientists for Global Responsibility New
Zealand. It's a 25-year-old charitable trust.
I have a BBus Agribusiness, Masters in Sociology.
My thesis was science policy, funding, and outcomes.
Now, the government has acknowledged that the risk assessment system is
outdated. Yet instead of updating risk assessment methods and
models first, it is legislating to make approvals easier, faster,
and less transparent. So in effect, members are being asked to
approve powers when the rules that underpin public trust and
confidence are not fit for purpose.
So the bill cannot be risk proportionate at this stage.
The regulatory impact statement problem definition concerns limited
access, lengthy, and uncertain approval pathways.
The RIS problem was not concerned with ensuring that the purpose and principles
of the HSNO Act would be upheld following any legislative
amendment. The members are not being asked to decide whether a
particular pathway is safe. Members are being asked to create pathways
before the underlying risk assessment evaluation has been
published, consulted on, and independently reviewed.
The HSNO Act amendment bill arose from the
recommendations made by the Ministry for Regulation,
Agricultural and Horticultural Products Regulatory Review, yet
that consultation was flawed. Back in 2024, the
Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment commented that the regulatory
review consultation itself was too narrow.
Officials then invited expert stakeholders into the
HSNO Amendment Bill consultation who stood to
financially benefit from deregulation.
So this includes former AgCam, the Animal and Plant Health Association of New
Zealand that are funded by the agrochemical industry, and the Crown
Research Institutes that MBIE funds to create
biotechnology. So the people consulted were largely
organizations whose institutional mission is to obtain approvals,
develop and commercialise biotechnology, or accelerate product
access. Missing were doctors, public health
experts, occupational disease experts, environmental toxicologists,
consumer representatives, the organic sector, and the communities bearing the
risks.
A 2017 Treasury document, "Government Expectations for
Good Regulatory Practice," requires that affected and interested
parties should be granted appropriate opportunities to comment.
So two flawed consultations to date have failed to do
this. The May 2025 RIS fails to observe
the Treasury Government Expectations document in other ways.
Treasury requires that systematic impact and risk
analysis is undertaken, and that genuine effort
is made to identify, understand, and estimate the various
categories of cost and benefit. So there's no
cost-benefit analysis in the RIS, astonishing, and the
only cost-benefit language concerns levy funding for the regulated
industries. So the government has stated that the EPA risk
assessment framework is outdated.
Recommendation 10 of the Hort and Ag review
stated, "Update the EPA's outdated risk assessment models
and consider how to keep them up to date for the future." But
until risk assessment processes and models are revised,
incorporating the PCE's advice on
environmental monitoring, risks cannot be understood
and costs cannot be understood. So the
process is upside down. The bill is
premature. Fix risk assessment first, then
legislate. So the Amendment Bill contains mechanisms that
deregulate two streams, both toxic chemical regulations and
GMO gene editing pathways. But the bill reduces
public notification, it removes the methodology requirements for temporary
approvals, it creates new undefined concepts,
and expands reliance on overseas decisions, and
concentrates more authority within the EPA.
But we're seeing scientific scrutiny decrease.
So there's an accountability problem.
Two overseas jurisdictions cannot assure good
decision-making. For example,
FAO/WHO decisions are
conventionally less rigorous than European Food Safety Authority
decisions, which also require public feedback.
Then there is the issue of recent decisions versus older
decisions.
Transparency. Public oversight is substantially eroded.
The bill concentrates authority and expands executive
discretion at the same time that it contracts scientific oversight.
Then there's an evidentiary standards problem.
So the bill enables the EPA to denew a new
organism without appropriate safeguards and scrutiny.
Denewing is the process of prescribing an organism as not
new so that it will no longer be regulated by the HASNO Act.
The legal term appears to be invented.
The MB officials met with NZ EPA to
discuss gene technology regulation.
The EPA explained that they weren't able to regulate newer
gene-edited, or deregulate newer gene-edited organisms
due to the 2014 High Court case with the Sustainability
Council. The denewing concept is central to the bill.
Overriding that 2014 finding without risk evaluation
or cost analysis is extraordinary.
So officials and Crown law experts are inventing terminology,
expanding regulatory powers, and reducing transparency, but
without corresponding safeguards, regulatory frameworks, review mechanisms,
transparency requirements. So it seems that they might view
regulatory risk assessment as a secondary issue and any obligation
to protect human and environmental health as lower order
importance.
New Zealand ag exports are valued at 64 billion.
Select committee members know this, that New Zealand exporters position
themselves as quality suppliers to secure sales in
competitive markets. The 2017 Treasury document
demands cost-benefit evaluation is undertaken, but the
RIS did not evaluate the premium afforded by non-GMO status and
the broader trust status afforded by non-GMO messaging.
Consumer preference applies no matter where on the
GMO spectrum a modified or an edited
organism sits. So whether you're just making insertions or
deletions inside the gene or you're adding foreign DNA,
it's still a GMO.
In Ag Researchers Select Committee presentation to the Gene Tech
Bill, they acknowledged that their GM grass, the GM livestock
feed, could not be contained. So one of the first
releases could potentially undermine the capacity for meat
exports to be declared GMO-free.
And MPs, you are right to be concerned about resilience and
productivity, but resilience is not a single gene trait.
It emerges from complex interactions between genetics, soils, nutritions,
weather, ecosystems, pest management.
At the same time, these challenges are intensifying.
MB has reduced funding pathways for much of the public
good science that needs to address them.
So deregulation is not a substitute for
scientific capability.
PSGR NZ requests that the Primary Production Committee pause proceedings and
return the bill for fundamental revision following genuine
consultation extending well beyond the narrowly selected groups engaged to
date, and this includes comprehensive evaluation to understand the current
state of New Zealand risk assessment, but also the evaluation of
international regimes so that we do not fall into the trap of
deferring to inferior jurisdictions.
So it's a question of sequence. No responsible
regulatory system designs new approval pathways
before the models those pathways will rely upon have
been validated.
First, establish a modern, transparent, scientifically robust risk
assessment framework with best practice models.
Then decide, through robust risk evaluation and
cost-benefit analysis, what approvals that framework
can safely support. So on pages 19 to 30 of our submission,
PSR speak directly to the clauses that are the most
problematic. And so we leave you with this question, if the risk assessment
framework is outdated and unfit for purpose, why are we expanding
approval pathways before we know what needs to be fixed?
I thank you very much for this time.
Thank you.

crop on around about 1,000 hectares over 37 properties spread out

We're very proud at Woodhaven that we use our wonderful natural
resources in terms of our
awa, our water, our whenua, our land, to grow healthy food that
predominantly stays here in New Zealand and feeds New Zealanders.
And I think it's really important that we understand the flow-on effects of this
legislation. So without safe and
effective means of controlling pest and disease, we literally cannot grow
the food that feeds this country.
So, when we have regulatory barriers to get new chemistry in,
it leads to old chemistry being overused and resistant
profile building up in crops for disease and pests.
And that means that we lower our yields continuously.
Now- The skeptics amongst some of us might turn around
and say, "Well, the reason that growers would advocate for this is because we get
to make more money." The real effect of this is what it
does to food supply, right? So if we grow

We want those controls to be safe, not just from a human food safety

So we obviously respect that there needs to be controls in place
to ensure that when we bring new solutions in, whether those are
bio solutions, whether those are synthetic or chemical-based
solutions, that they are both safe and effective
for human beings and for the environment.
But we need a process that actually delivers those in a timely manner and delivers
new solutions to growers in order to be able to
maintain or even expand yield. Now, I think one of the things that's
overlooked in this process a lot is
that actually by having effective control measures for pest and
disease, we're actually able to lower the overall environmental
effect of food production.
And I'll explain why. If we need 100
cabbages to feed the country and I only get a 50% yield,
I have to grow 200 cabbages, and I have to put on the nitrate
fertilizer for 200 cabbages. I have to apply the water for 200 cabbages.
I have to cultivate land for 200 cabbages.
And our biggest effect, environmental effect of vegetable growing is sediment loss
and it's nutrient loss. Now, if I've got really effective control
mechanisms for pest and disease, and it means that I only have to grow
110 cabbages to get my 100 cabbages to feed the country, I'm
actually going to dramatically lower my overall environmental effect.
So that's why these policies are really important, not just from an
economic perspective in terms of exports, not just in terms of a food
security perspective for our country, but also
mental perspective, because the more we can get effective
control of pest and disease, the higher our yield is, the less
crop we actually need in the ground to effectively feed everybody.
So this is a massive win-win piece of legislation if we get
this right, and there is fantastic advancements in this
space around new chemistry, around new bio
controls that will allow us to get better yields in this country and deliver
better outcomes for growers and all New Zealanders and the environment.
